resources.string: Choose whether Diagnostics should validate the current path or run isolated strategy trials that return a manual recommendation.
Automatic probing does not change your RIPDPI settings. Review the recommended strategy below, then copy the parts you want into your normal profile manually.
Automatic probing is already running. Wait for it to finish before starting another diagnostics run.
Validate DNS, HTTP, HTTPS, and TCP reachability with either a raw-path scan or the currently running in-path service.
Diagnostics is validating the selected path right now. The latest probe results and session summary will update when the run finishes.
Run isolated HTTP, HTTPS, and QUIC strategy trials against the current network. RIPDPI returns a recommendation, but does not apply it automatically.
RIPDPI is launching temporary strategy candidates on raw path only. Your saved settings stay unchanged while probing runs.
The latest automatic probing run finished and produced a manual recommendation with TCP and QUIC winners.
Automatic probing only works with visual RIPDPI settings. Turn off raw command-line mode before running this workflow.

Run the full built-in HTTP, HTTPS, and QUIC strategy matrix against the current network. RIPDPI records what worked, what partially worked, what failed, and what was not applicable without changing saved settings.
RIPDPI is running the full built-in strategy matrix on raw path only. The current VPN or proxy session is paused and restored around the audit.
The latest automatic audit finished with a full matrix, per-candidate evidence, and a manual recommendation.
Automatic audit only works with visual RIPDPI settings. Turn off raw command-line mode before running this workflow.
Region-focused web connectivity, DNS, TLS, and HTTP evidence.
Messaging bootstrap, media, and protocol reachability checks.
Tor and VPN bootstrap plus handshake reachability.
Manual throughput windows against neutral control endpoints.
Combined region-specific network heuristics across multiple services.
Manual local checks tuned for regional network patterns. Results are evidence, not attribution.
Confidence is moderate. Review the candidate evidence before copying this strategy into your main settings.
The audit recommendation is still available, but the matrix coverage or winner strength was weak. Review per-candidate evidence before applying it manually.
Tap a winner for per-target evidence and configuration details.
Tap a candidate for per-target evidence and configuration details.
Start RIPDPI at least once to capture durable runtime sessions, samples, and failures.
Run a raw-path or in-path scan to persist reports and probe details here.
Runtime failures and native events will appear here once RIPDPI records them.
Persistent history moved out of Diagnostics
Use the dedicated History screen for saved connection sessions, previous scans, and the durable event feed.
Build a concise incident brief from the latest or selected diagnostics session and send it through the Android share sheet.

Build a ZIP with the focused session, strategy matrix, snapshots, contexts, recent live telemetry, global runtime events, and the app-visible logcat snapshot, then send it directly through the Android share sheet.
Save %1$s locally. The archive keeps raw report data and the app-visible logcat snapshot, but redacts network identity fields in summary and manifest files.
Export the same app-visible logcat snapshot as a plain-text file when you need just the raw local trace.
Start RIPDPI when you want to route traffic through this device.
Grant Android VPN permission if prompted. Setup continues on-device.
Traffic is currently routed through the selected local mode.
Review the message below and try again.
%1$s Use the connection button to continue the permission flow.
%1$s Open Settings or use the button below to repair access.
Open Diagnostics and review approach analytics
History keeps durable connection sessions, sampled runtime diagnostics, and native failures outside the live Diagnostics tools.
Balanced defaults for most networks. Keeps the local VPN path and safe RIPDPI tuning.
Use the local proxy path with the same baseline tuning when VPN mode is not desired.
Manual tuning for DNS, proxy endpoint, engine values, and optional command line overrides.
Local VPN or proxy routing path
Saved now and applied the next time local VPN mode is active
Where the local proxy listener binds
Editing %1$s. Changes validate immediately and are stored on this device.
Used for the local VPN route.
DNS override is only applied in local VPN mode.
Where the local proxy listener binds.
Accepted range follows the current service port validation.
Upper bound for concurrent local sessions.
Leave empty to use the engine default.

Use [tcp] and [udp] sections. Markers accept numeric or semantic offsets. The HTTPS section below can author single tlsrec and randomized tlsrandrec steps visually, while the Hostfake and fake approximation cards explain hostname-targeted fake chunks and retransmission-style approximations. Linux/Android-only approximations are available as fakedsplit host+1 and fakeddisorder endhost for HTTP or TLS SNI-adjacent payloads.
Use [tcp] and [udp] sections with numeric or semantic offset markers.

The HTTPS section below can author single tlsrec and randomized tlsrandrec steps visually, while the Hostfake and fake approximation cards explain hostname-targeted fake chunks and retransmission-style approximations. Linux/Android-only approximations are available as fakedsplit host+1 and fakeddisorder endhost for HTTP or TLS SNI-adjacent payloads.
Manual markers accept offsets like 1, host, method+2, midsld-1, or 1+s. Use the adaptive preset selector or the raw chain DSL for auto(\u2026).
Accepts numeric or semantic offsets like 0, host+1, endhost-2, or 1+se. Adaptive auto(\u2026) markers are not supported here.
0 = input-sized, positive = exact target bytes, negative = input minus N.
Accepts offsets like 0, host, sniext, extlen, or 1+s.
UDP fake bursts now live inside the strategy chain editor as fake_burst counts.
Configure the TLS prelude that rewrites HTTPS ClientHello records before send-step actions run. The chain editor above still handles stacked or mixed TLS preludes.
Choose whether HTTPS ClientHello traffic stays untouched, splits once at a marker, or fans out into randomized tail records.
Do not add a TLS prelude step. RIPDPI keeps the outgoing ClientHello record layout intact.
Add one tlsrec step and split the ClientHello record once at the selected marker.
Add one tlsrandrec step. RIPDPI keeps an optional prefix, then emits randomized tail records after the marker.
Visual TLS record fragmentation controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use command line chain flags for the next start.
A TLS prelude step is saved, but RIPDPI will ignore it until HTTPS desync is enabled again.
The current chain starts with multiple tlsrec or tlsrandrec steps. This visual editor shows the first step and will collapse the stack into one saved profile when you edit it.
RIPDPI will leave HTTPS ClientHello records untouched unless you add a TLS prelude step.
The running RIPDPI session started before this TLS prelude change. Reconnect or restart to apply the saved profile.
RIPDPI will split each matching ClientHello into one prefix record and one tail record at the selected marker.
RIPDPI will rebuild each matching ClientHello into randomized tail records after the selected marker.
Not used while TLS fragmentation is off
One split point. Emits a prefix record and one tail record.
Defined by the current raw RIPDPI command line profile.
Enable HTTPS desync before this profile can run.
Visual edits replace the current TLS prelude stack with one selected mode.
Applies before TCP send steps on matching HTTPS ClientHello traffic.
This visual editor manages one TLS prelude profile. Saving here replaces any stacked tlsrec or tlsrandrec prefix with the selected mode.
Need stacked tlsrec and tlsrandrec steps, repeat markers, or mixed prelude order? Use the chain editor above. Saving here always collapses the TLS prelude prefix to one visual profile.
How many randomized tail TLS records RIPDPI should emit after the selected marker. Allowed range: 2 to 16.
Lower byte bound for each randomized tail record. Allowed range: 1 to 4096 bytes.
Upper byte bound for each randomized tail record. Must stay between the saved minimum and 4096 bytes.
Maximum fragment size must be between the saved minimum and 4096 bytes.
Remember the winning TCP route per HTTP Host or TLS SNI and prefer it on future connections.
How long a failing host route stays deprioritized before RIPDPI can prefer it again.
Maximum number of remembered hosts stored on this device.
TCP HTTP/TLS only. Learned hosts are remembered across restarts.
Store a validated routing policy for the current network fingerprint and reuse it on future starts.
Only validated network matches auto-apply. Raw SSID, BSSID, and carrier identifiers stay out of storage.
Controls when Telegram traffic uses WSS tunnel via web.telegram.org.

Visual host learning settings are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --host-autolearn, --host-autolearn-penalty-ttl, --host-autolearn-max-hosts, and optional --host-autolearn-file in command line settings.
This session is learning hosts through command line flags. Edit raw RIPDPI arguments to change the next start.
RIPDPI is currently remembering winning TCP routes for HTTP Host and TLS SNI traffic on this device.
Host learning is enabled in settings, but the current RIPDPI session started before this change.
The running RIPDPI session is still using host learning from an earlier start.
Host learning will begin the next time RIPDPI starts.
A learned-host store is already present on this device. Clear it if you want RIPDPI to rebuild host preferences from scratch.
RIPDPI will use normal route selection without remembering hosts.
Stored host routes are available on this device.
Forget is unavailable here because command line settings can point RIPDPI at a different learned-host file.
Nothing is stored yet. RIPDPI writes the learned-host file after it successfully learns a host.
No stored host routes are available to clear yet.
Clearing the stored file does not change the active session until RIPDPI restarts.
Use this if you want the next RIPDPI start to relearn every host from scratch.

Gate the whole active desync group by outbound round, payload size, and cumulative stream-byte position. Leave fields empty to keep that dimension unbounded. Per-step filters still belong in the chain DSL via when_round, when_size, and when_stream.
Visual activation-window controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --round, --payload-size-range, and --stream-byte-range in command line settings for the next start.
No active desync chain is using the group activation window right now. You can still preconfigure it for the next time group-wide desync is enabled.
Saved, but the group is off
A custom activation window is saved, but no active desync group is using it until group-wide desync is turned back on.
The running RIPDPI session keeps its current group gating. Restart RIPDPI to apply this saved activation window.
RIPDPI will gate the whole active desync group by the ranges below before it emits fake, split, TLS prelude, or UDP fake-burst behavior.
The group itself stays always-on, but some TCP or UDP steps still carry when_round, when_size, or when_stream filters inside the chain DSL.
The active desync group can run on every outbound chunk unless a step-level DSL filter narrows it further.
Command line settings decide which outbound chunks are eligible for group-wide desync.
No active desync group is currently consuming this gate.
Only outbound chunks inside the saved ranges can use group-wide desync, fake transport, TLS prelude, and UDP fake bursts.
Every outbound chunk can use the active desync group.
No step-local when_round, when_size, or when_stream rules are saved in the chain DSL.
%1$d step rules in the chain DSL still add their own activation filters on top of the group window.
Only selected outbound payload rounds can activate the whole desync group.
Current outbound chunk size in bytes.
Only outbound chunks whose size falls in this range can activate the whole desync group.
Cumulative outbound byte positions for the current chunk.
Only outbound chunks that touch these cumulative byte positions can activate the whole desync group.

This visual editor controls the first non-TLS TCP send step. Use Manual marker for explicit offsets, or switch to an adaptive preset that resolves per payload from live TCP MSS hints and semantic markers.
The current first TCP send step is hostfake. Keep using the chain DSL if you need to edit its boundary.
Rewrites the first non-TLS TCP send step in the saved chain.
Rewrites the legacy split marker used to synthesize the primary send step.
Command line settings decide the effective split placement.
No active TCP send step is using this visual editor right now.
The current first TCP send step is hostfake, so adaptive placement stays in the raw chain DSL.
The selected adaptive preset resolves one concrete split boundary per payload from live TCP transport hints and semantic markers.
The current first TCP send step uses an explicit saved marker.
Visual adaptive split controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use explicit markers or auto(\u2026) in command line chain arguments for the next start.
The current first TCP send step is hostfake, which keeps its own explicit marker. Use the chain DSL when you want to adjust that boundary.
An adaptive marker is saved for the primary split step, but no active TCP send step is using it until desync is enabled again.
The current primary TCP send step uses an explicit marker instead of adaptive placement.
The running RIPDPI session started before this adaptive split change. Restart or reconnect to apply the saved preset.
RIPDPI will resolve the primary TCP split marker per payload from live TCP hints and semantic markers.
The primary TCP split step keeps using the explicit saved marker below.
Known explicit offsets like host+1, endhost, or sniext+4.
Advanced adaptive markers from the raw chain DSL, including presets not exposed in the visual editor.
General-purpose HTTP and HTTPS traffic when you want RIPDPI to follow live segment size hints.
Splitting right at the first Host or SNI bytes.
Pushing the tail of the hostname or SNI into the next write.
TLS ClientHello layouts where the SNI extension boundary matters more than the hostname text itself.
HTTP and HTTPS visual desync flows.
No active HTTP or HTTPS visual desync flow is enabled right now.
Use the chain DSL for auto(method), auto(midsld), and auto(extlen), or when hostfake owns the first send step.
Visual presets only rewrite the first non-TLS TCP send step. TLS-prelude steps and secondary send steps stay in the raw chain DSL.
Keep an explicit saved marker for the primary TCP send step. Good when you already know the exact boundary you want.
Recommended default. Biases toward the nearest semantic boundary around the live MSS-sized target for each payload.
Prefers the start of HTTP Host or TLS SNI. Useful when the network reacts to the first hostname bytes.
Prefers the end of HTTP Host or TLS SNI. Useful when you want the hostname tail moved into the next segment.
HTTPS-focused. Prefers the TLS SNI extension boundary before falling back to other semantic markers.
The raw chain DSL currently uses %1$s for the primary step. Pick any preset below to replace it in the visual editor.
Control whether RIPDPI decrypts QUIC Initial packets to recover SNI for UDP host routing, filters, cache records, and telemetry.
Disabled skips QUIC inspection. Route by SNI uses the recovered hostname for live routing only. Route and cache also stores QUIC-derived hosts for repeated UDP flows.
Allow hostname extraction from QUIC version 1 Initial packets.
Allow hostname extraction from QUIC version 2 Initial packets.
Pick the payload RIPDPI should emit during UDP fake_burst steps when the original traffic is a QUIC Initial.
Only applies to the realistic preset. Leave it empty to use the built-in fake host inside the generated QUIC Initial.
Use a plain domain like video.example.org. IP literals and invalid hostnames are ignored.
The realistic preset will keep using the RIPDPI built-in fake host until you save a custom override.
The realistic preset will rewrite the generated QUIC Initial SNI to match this hostname.
QUIC routing and cache toggles above still control hostname extraction. This preset only changes the fake payload emitted by UDP fake_burst.
Keep the existing raw UDP fake payload path. Use this when you only want transport-level fake bursts.
Emit a fixed 620-byte compatibility blob for QUIC fake payloads.
Build a valid IETF QUIC Initial, mirror v1 or v2 when possible, and optionally override the embedded host.
Visual QUIC fake payload settings are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --fake-quic-profile and --fake-quic-host in command line settings for the next start.
Enable UDP desync if you want RIPDPI to emit fake QUIC Initial packets during fake_burst steps.
RIPDPI will keep using the current raw UDP fake payload path even when fake_burst is configured.
Profile saved for UDP fake bursts
The QUIC fake profile is configured, but the current UDP chain has no fake_burst step yet. Add fake_burst in the chain editor to emit it.
Restart to apply QUIC fake payloads
The running RIPDPI session started before this QUIC fake payload change. Restart RIPDPI to apply the new profile.
RIPDPI will use the selected fake QUIC Initial profile on the next fake_burst that targets QUIC Initial traffic.
Applies only when the UDP chain includes fake_burst.
Applies to UDP fake_burst on QUIC Initial traffic only.
%1$d fake_burst packets configured by the UDP chain.
No fake_burst step is configured in the UDP chain yet.
Choose the built-in HTTP, TLS, and generic UDP payload shapes RIPDPI should use when no raw custom fake blob is provided. QUIC Initial presets stay in the QUIC section below.
These presets are ready to save now and apply when the current strategy emits fake packets later.
Base payload for HTTP fake packets. It only changes the believable request shape, not the transport behavior.
Base fake ClientHello before RIPDPI applies Fake TLS SNI, randomization, Session ID, padding, or size tuning.
Base payload for generic UDP fake_burst traffic. QUIC Initial fake payloads are still controlled separately below.
Visual fake payload presets are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Command line flags or --fake-data will decide the effective payloads on the next start.
HTTP, HTTPS, and UDP desync are all disabled, so these presets are currently idle.
Restart to apply fake payload presets
RIPDPI is running with an earlier payload library state. Restart to apply the selected HTTP, TLS, and UDP fake payload presets.
At least one protocol is using a non-default built-in fake payload profile.
RIPDPI is using the compatibility fake payload defaults for HTTP, TLS, and UDP.
Command line settings choose the effective payload source.
All protocol toggles are currently off.
Full fake TCP packets and generic UDP fake_burst packets are live in the current strategy.
Full fake TCP packets are live in the current strategy.
Generic UDP fake_burst packets are live in the current strategy.
Current traffic shaping uses hostfake chunks, so full fake payload presets are saved for later.
Used by fake TCP and generic UDP payload generation when no raw fake blob override is present.
HTTP desync is disabled, so this profile is currently idle.
Used for full fake HTTP packets on the current chain.
Current HTTP traffic uses hostfake chunks instead of a full fake payload.
Ready when the TCP chain adds a full fake step for HTTP traffic.
Parser-only, split, disorder, and hostfake steps do not consume this full HTTP payload profile.
HTTPS desync is disabled, so this base ClientHello stays idle.
Used for fake HTTPS ClientHello packets on the current chain.
Current HTTPS traffic uses hostfake chunks instead of a full fake ClientHello.
Ready when the TCP chain adds a full fake HTTPS step.
Fake TLS SNI, randomization, Session ID duplication, padding, and size tuning layer on top of this base profile.
UDP desync is disabled, so this generic fake payload is currently idle.
Used for %1$d generic UDP fake_burst packets on the current chain.
Ready when the UDP chain adds fake_burst for generic UDP traffic.
QUIC Initial payloads stay in the separate QUIC fake profile section below.
QUIC Initial traffic uses the QUIC fake profile below, while this profile covers the remaining generic UDP fake_burst traffic.
Prefer raw RIPDPI flags over the visual editor.
Applied when command line settings are enabled.
Keep sampling connection telemetry, network state, and context while RIPDPI is running.
Enable runtime diagnostics history to edit sampling cadence.
How often RIPDPI stores network, telemetry, and context samples for an active connection. Allowed range: 5 to 300 seconds.
How long saved connection sessions, samples, diagnostics reports, and export records stay on-device before cleanup.
Attach stored history tables when building a diagnostics archive for sharing or local export.
Reset the per-install salt used to hash network fingerprints. Previously recorded hashes can no longer be correlated with future sessions.
Arguments below take precedence over the visual controls on this screen.
Check the highlighted fields before saving.
The editor is validating in real time. Fix the marked fields, then save again.
Enter a valid non-local DNS address.
Enter a valid proxy listen address.
Value is outside the allowed range.
Enter a valid strategy chain DSL.

[tcp] tlsrec extlen hostfake endhost+8 midhost=midsld host=googlevideo.com split midsld
[udp] fake_burst 3
Add a hostfake step when you want fake chunks only around the HTTP Host or TLS SNI span instead of faking the whole payload.
The saved strategy chain already contains hostfake steps and will use them on the next RIPDPI start.
Hostfake is configured in settings, but the running RIPDPI session started before this chain change.
Enable HTTP or HTTPS desync if you want hostfake to target Host or SNI fields.
Visual hostfake guidance stays visible here, but the active chain comes from raw RIPDPI arguments while command line settings are enabled.
Keep the real host as one span
Hostfake only touches Host/SNI-sized chunks. It does not use the full Fake TLS profile or fake offset marker.
Add fakedsplit or fakeddisorder when you want a Linux/Android approximation of fake-backed retransmission ambiguity without raw sequence overlap.
The saved strategy chain already contains fakedsplit or fakeddisorder steps and will reuse the current fake payload, fake TLS, and fake TTL profiles on the next RIPDPI start.
The running RIPDPI session started before this chain change, so fakedsplit and fakeddisorder will not apply until RIPDPI reconnects or restarts.
Enable HTTP or HTTPS desync if you want fakedsplit or fakeddisorder around request or ClientHello regions.
Visual fake-approximation guidance stays visible here, but the active chain comes from raw RIPDPI arguments while command line settings are enabled.
Ready when the chain adds fakedsplit or fakeddisorder
Use the step marker from the chain DSL
Reuses the full fake payload library, Fake TLS layers, fake offset, and fixed or adaptive fake TTL.
No approximation step is active yet.
Two fake-backed regions. Each side of the split relies on one fake send plus the later original retransmission.
TTL 1 hides the first region from the remote path at first, then the second region uses the configured fake transport.
These are Linux/Android-first approximations, not raw wire-parity implementations. They reuse RIPDPI fake payloads and fake transport instead of sequence overlap or duplicate raw sends.
Encrypted DNS intercepts DNS inside the VPN and resolves it through the selected encrypted protocol. Plain DNS uses the IP address below directly.
Intercept DNS inside the VPN and resolve it through DoH, DoT, or DNSCrypt.
Send DNS directly to the resolver IP without encrypted interception or bootstrap setup.
Choose which encrypted DNS transport RIPDPI should use for VPN DNS interception and diagnostics.
Best default workflow. Built-in Cloudflare, Google, Quad9, and AdGuard profiles are ready to use.
DNS-over-TLS on port 853. Good when you control the hostname and TLS server name.
DNSCrypt with provider-name and public-key validation. Use this for custom resolvers only.
Use custom fields when you need your own endpoint, hostname, TLS name, or DNSCrypt credentials.
Set a specific resolver address for the local VPN path and keep it stored on this device.
Use a custom DNS-over-HTTPS endpoint with explicit bootstrap IPs so tunnel resolution does not depend on plain DNS.
Use an HTTPS endpoint that accepts RFC 8484 DNS messages.
Enter a valid HTTPS DoH endpoint.
Use a DNS-over-TLS endpoint with an explicit host, port, TLS name, and bootstrap IPs.
Hostname or IP for the DNS-over-TLS endpoint.
Server name used during TLS certificate verification.
Use a DNSCrypt resolver by specifying the endpoint, provider name, public key, and bootstrap IPs.
Hostname or IP for the DNSCrypt server.
Certificate provider name advertised by the DNSCrypt resolver.
Resolver signing key as 64 hexadecimal characters.
Enter a valid 64-character hexadecimal public key.
Use the resolver port for the selected encrypted protocol.
Enter one or more resolver IPs separated by commas or spaces.
Enter one or more non-local bootstrap IP addresses.
Save this form to replace the current built-in profile with a custom endpoint.
Not required in plain DNS mode
Mapped DNS interception is active in local VPN mode.
Queries go directly to this resolver IP in local VPN mode.
Saved now and applied the next time local VPN mode is active.
DNS override is only applied while RIPDPI is running in local VPN mode.
Allow the local VPN builder to include IPv6 DNS routing when the selected network supports it.
Fast public resolver with a minimal setup footprint.
Widely reachable public resolver with familiar defaults.
Security-focused public resolver with threat blocking.
Public resolver that emphasizes ad and tracker blocking.
Review the latest local DNS, connection, warning, and error activity captured by RIPDPI on this device.
No local events have been captured yet.
Filter by subsystem or severity to focus on the runtime you are debugging.
Keep the latest matching entry in view while new diagnostics arrive.
Show the current connection or diagnostics session first and hide older persisted history.
Start RIPDPI or trigger a diagnostic event to populate the on-device stream.
No entries match the current filters
Adjust the type chips above to inspect a different part of the local buffer.
RIPDPI works locally. No remote servers, no accounts, no data leaves your phone.
Android will ask to create a local VPN. This routes traffic through the app on your device, and nothing is sent externally.
Start with a preset or configure manually. You can change everything later.
Choose whether RIPDPI should keep one fixed fake TTL or learn a working TCP fake TTL inside the saved window during the current run.
Keep one saved TTL for TCP fake packets, hostfake chunks, and disoob writes.
Recommended. Start from the fallback TTL, search lower values first inside the saved window, and pin the first working TTL for the current run.
The saved profile uses a custom adaptive bias of %1$d. Pick Fixed TTL or Adaptive TTL to replace it in the visual editor.
Visual adaptive fake TTL controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. The active fake TTL logic comes from your command line profile.
Use one saved TTL for TCP fake packets only. UDP fake bursts keep their own fixed behavior.
An adaptive fake TTL profile is stored, but no current TCP fake transport step is using it until fake, hostfake, or disoob traffic becomes active again.
Restart to apply adaptive fake TTL
The running RIPDPI session started before this adaptive fake TTL change. Restart or reconnect to apply the saved learning window.
RIPDPI will learn a working TCP fake TTL inside the saved window during the current run and pin it after success.
RIPDPI will keep the imported custom adaptive bias and learn TCP fake TTL values inside the saved window during the current run.
Command line settings choose the effective fake TTL logic.
No active TCP fake packets, hostfake chunks, or disoob writes are using this profile right now.
Used only for TCP fake packets, hostfake chunks, and disoob writes. UDP fake bursts stay fixed.
Used as the saved fixed TTL for TCP fake packets, hostfake chunks, and disoob writes.
No active TCP fake transport step
Learned during the current run only. Successful TTLs are not persisted across restarts.
No learning. RIPDPI always uses the saved fixed TTL.
Lower edge of the adaptive TCP fake TTL search window.
Upper edge of the adaptive TCP fake TTL search window.
Seed TTL for adaptive learning and the fallback value if the current run does not pin a better TTL.
Tune how RIPDPI builds fake HTTPS ClientHello packets before they are split or sent.
Fake TLS settings are idle until the current strategy chain includes an HTTPS fake step.
Default uses RIPDPI’s built-in fake ClientHello. Original starts from the real outgoing ClientHello before applying fake-TLS mods.
Fixed keeps the configured fake SNI. Randomized generates a same-length hostname on each fake HTTPS packet.
Randomize the ClientHello random, Session ID, and KeyShare bytes in the fake packet.
Copy the Session ID from the real ClientHello when the lengths match.
Adjust TLS length fields so the real payload fits inside the fake packet’s padding accounting.
Visual fake TLS controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --fake-tls-mod, --fake-sni, and the rest of your command line profile for the next start.
The fake TLS profile is stored on this device, but RIPDPI will ignore it until HTTPS desync is enabled again.
A custom fake TLS profile is ready, but the current TCP chain does not include a fake step that can send a fake ClientHello.
Add a fake TCP step when you want RIPDPI to shape fake HTTPS ClientHello packets with the profile below.
RIPDPI will use the tuned fake ClientHello profile below whenever the current TCP chain emits fake HTTPS packets.
RIPDPI will use its default fake ClientHello with the profile below whenever the current TCP chain emits fake HTTPS packets.
Randomized hostname with the same length as the configured fake SNI
Randomize Random, Session ID, and KeyShare
Copy Session ID from the original ClientHello
Defined by the current raw RIPDPI command line profile.
Enable HTTPS desync before this profile can run.
Requires a fake TCP step in the strategy chain.
Used for HTTPS fake packets when the current visual strategy emits them.
Used for HTTPS fake packets after RIPDPI reconnects or restarts.
Resets the fake TLS profile to RIPDPI’s default ClientHello, fixed SNI, and input-sized envelope.

HTTP parser tweaks only affect visual HTTP desync flows. Keep the safe group on for low-risk parser nudges, and enable the aggressive EOL evasions only when you are testing stubborn Nginx-biased DPI paths.
Visual HTTP parser-evasion controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Command line flags decide the active parser mutations on the next start.
HTTP desync is not active right now, so these parser tweaks are saved for later and do not affect current traffic.
Custom HTTP parser evasions are saved, but they stay idle until HTTP desync is turned back on.
The running RIPDPI session started before this HTTP parser change. Restart or reconnect to apply the saved parser evasions.
RIPDPI will apply the selected Nginx-biased EOL evasions together with any safe parser tweaks on HTTP traffic.
RIPDPI will apply the selected low-risk Host header tweaks on HTTP traffic.
No extra HTTP parser evasions are enabled. RIPDPI will keep HTTP requests structurally normal unless you switch on tweaks below.
Command line settings choose the active HTTP parser mutations.
No live HTTP visual desync flow is using these parser tweaks right now.
The saved parser tweaks apply after RIPDPI reconnects or restarts.
Used by the current HTTP visual desync flow when RIPDPI mutates outgoing requests.
Safer HTTP parser tweaks that preserve normal request structure while nudging brittle DPI parsers off the happy path.
Aggressive Nginx-biased evasions. These can bypass weaker HTTP parsers, but they may also break some servers and CDNs.
Automatic probing can test the safe parser profile plus separate Unix EOL and Method EOL candidates.
Connection timed out. Please try again.
Pick a ready-made service list for common apps with connectivity issues. Refresh can download a newer geosite snapshot, while Apply writes the selected pack into the blacklist or whitelist below.
RIPDPI host filters match by suffix. Converted full: geosite entries therefore apply to the exact host and its subdomains.
Choose where this curated host list should go. Only the selected text field changes.
RIPDPI is using the curated host-pack snapshot shipped with the app. Refresh downloads the latest geosite.dat, verifies its SHA-256 checksum, and only then replaces the on-device cache.
The current curated host packs came from a checksum-verified geosite.dat download stored on this device.
RIPDPI is downloading geosite.dat from the geosite repository and will only replace the local cache after SHA-256 verification succeeds.
Source: raw.githubusercontent.com geosite repository release/geosite.dat plus its published SHA-256 file.
Uncheck all to desync all traffic
Visual controls are unavailable while command line overrides are active.
Enable command line settings to edit raw RIPDPI flags here.
OOB data must be empty or a single character.
Android will ask to create a local VPN. This routes traffic through the app on your device — nothing is sent externally.
Grant Android VPN permission to keep RIPDPI running locally on this device.
Notification permission is required before RIPDPI can start its local VPN or proxy.
Notification permission is still blocked. Open app settings and allow notifications for RIPDPI.
Allow RIPDPI to ignore Android battery optimization so Doze is less likely to interrupt the local VPN or proxy in the background.
Some devices add extra background limits beyond Android battery optimization. If RIPDPI stops in the background, review the app battery or background usage settings.

Samsung devices can still stop RIPDPI in the background even when Android battery optimization is disabled. If sessions stop, set App info > Battery to Unrestricted and add RIPDPI to Never sleeping apps.
Xiaomi/MIUI devices may block RIPDPI from running in the background. Go to Settings > Apps > Manage apps > RIPDPI, set Battery saver to No restrictions and enable Autostart.
Huawei devices may stop RIPDPI in the background. Go to Settings > Battery > App launch, find RIPDPI, disable automatic management, and enable Auto-launch, Secondary launch, and Run in background.

OnePlus devices may aggressively stop background apps. Go to Settings > Battery > Battery optimization, find RIPDPI and select Don\'t optimize. Also disable Deep Optimization and Sleep Standby Optimization if available.
Foreground service notifications are available for RIPDPI.
Required before Android can start the local VPN or proxy foreground service.
Required for the currently selected local VPN mode.
Only needed when you want to run RIPDPI in local VPN mode.
Grant Android VPN consent before RIPDPI can start the local VPN path.
Android is already letting RIPDPI ignore Doze battery optimization.
Recommended so Doze is less likely to pause RIPDPI while it is running in the background.
Resolver mode, protocol, IPv6, and local tunnel DNS behavior.
Saved now and applied the next time VPN mode is active.
Low-level RIPDPI flags, proxy listeners, and protocol overrides.
Reduce peer-discovery leaks that can bypass the local tunnel.
Biometric unlock is enabled with a local backup PIN fallback.
Biometric unlock is enabled. Add a backup PIN for fallback access.
Ask for biometric authentication before opening the dashboard.
You\'ll need to verify your identity each time you open the dashboard.
Set a four-digit fallback PIN so you can still unlock RIPDPI if biometrics are unavailable.
Store a four-digit PIN on this device for fallback authentication.
A four-digit fallback PIN is already stored locally.
Backup PIN must be exactly four digits.
Choose whether the app follows the system theme or stays fixed.
The interface updates immediately after you switch this preference.
App icon, adaptive shape, and themed icon options.
Build a diagnostics ZIP with app-visible logcat and recent debug information, then send it through the Android share sheet.
Changing the app icon may require your launcher to refresh. The icon update can take a few seconds to appear on your home screen.
Launcher-controlled mask used for this app icon on the current device.
Your launcher decides the final icon mask. RIPDPI can switch icon artwork, but the home app keeps control of the visible shape.
Different launchers can show circle, squircle, rounded-square, or custom icon masks.
If the icon artwork changes, your launcher may need a refresh before the new mask preview appears.
Use biometric authentication before opening the main dashboard on this device.
Only biometric authentication is configured for this device right now.
Use the fallback PIN stored on this device if biometric authentication is unavailable.
Digits only. The PIN is checked locally and never leaves this device.
The backup PIN does not match.
Compose-based local configuration surface for the preserved RIPDPI engine, service, and DataStore stack.
RIPDPI builds on the open-source hufrea/ripdpi project and keeps all routing and configuration local to this device.
What data is collected, how it is stored, and your privacy controls.
Network diagnostics: transport type, signal strength, DNS configuration
DPI detection results: failure classifications, strategy effectiveness
Performance metrics: RTT measurements, retry counts, traffic statistics
Network identity: hashed fingerprint of network characteristics (WiFi SSID, carrier info)
Device context: Android version, device model, app version, permission states
Optional: public IP address (only during manual diagnostic scans)
No browsing history or URL content
No personal data, accounts, or credentials
No data is sent to external servers automatically
No analytics, crash reporting, or advertising SDKs
All data stays on your device in a local database
You can disable passive monitoring in Advanced Settings
Export archives are only created when you explicitly request them
Exported archives redact IP addresses, WiFi identifiers, and other PII
You control what is shared and with whom
RIPDPI downloaded geosite.dat, verified its SHA-256 checksum, and updated the on-device curated host packs.
The downloaded geosite.dat checksum did not match the published SHA-256 digest. RIPDPI kept the current curated host packs.
RIPDPI verified the download, but the remote geosite.dat did not produce a complete YouTube, Telegram, and Discord catalog. The current packs remain in use.
RIPDPI could not download the latest geosite.dat or checksum file. The current curated host packs remain in use.
RIPDPI could not delete the stored host learning file on this device.
Learned hosts cleared for next start
The stored host learning file was removed. The running proxy keeps its in-memory routes until RIPDPI restarts.
Stored host learning was removed and the next RIPDPI start will rebuild it from scratch.
RIPDPI removed all stored per-network routing policies. Current settings remain unchanged.
Fake TLS profile reset for next start
The profile is back to the default fake ClientHello. Restart RIPDPI to apply it to the active session.
RIPDPI will use the default fake ClientHello, fixed SNI, and input-sized payload on the next start.
Adaptive fake TTL reset for next start
RIPDPI will go back to a fixed fake TTL for the active group after the next restart or reconnect.
RIPDPI will stop learning fake TTL values and use the fixed fake TTL on the next start.
Fake payload presets reset for next start
HTTP, TLS, and UDP fake payloads are back to compatibility defaults. Restart RIPDPI to apply them to the active session.
RIPDPI will use the compatibility fake payload defaults for HTTP, TLS, and UDP on the next start.
HTTP parser evasions reset for next start
HTTP parser tweaks are back to their defaults. Restart RIPDPI to remove the saved parser evasions from the active session.
RIPDPI will stop applying saved HTTP parser tweaks and EOL evasions on the next start.
Activation window reset for next start
The active desync group is back to always-on gating. Restart RIPDPI to remove the saved activation window from the running session.
RIPDPI will stop gating the active desync group by round, payload size, or stream-byte position on the next start.
A new salt will be generated on the next connection. Previously recorded fingerprint hashes can no longer be correlated.
VPN permission is not currently granted.
Notification permission is disabled, so service issues may be harder to notice.
Power or background restrictions may interfere with stable diagnostics.
Waiting for the next diagnostics session
Passive telemetry and the latest report do not show critical failures. Review live counters for drift.
Warnings or partial failures were detected. Compare the latest scan outcome with the current live monitor.
Recent events or scan outcomes indicate degradation, failures, or transport instability.
Start RIPDPI or run a scan to populate diagnostics analytics.
Continuous monitor is waiting for an active RIPDPI session.
%1$s only works with visual RIPDPI settings. Command-line mode is active.
%1$s starts a temporary raw-path RIPDPI runtime and returns a manual recommendation.
%1$s is raw-path only because it launches isolated temporary strategy trials.
%1$s is blocked because %2$s is enabled. Turn it off in Advanced Settings before running this workflow so RIPDPI can launch isolated temporary strategy trials with the visual configuration.
Select a diagnostics session to generate a summary.
Archive includes the focused diagnostics session in full.
It also adds recent live telemetry and global runtime events.
Summary and manifest redact support context and network identity fields, while raw report data stays intact.
Archive includes approach analytics for %1$s with %2$s validation and runtime health context.

The app stopped unexpectedly due to:
%1$s

Would you like to share the crash report with the developer?
